next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

Noreen had wrapped Elsie's blue-enamel saucepan in a clean tea towel as if it were something fragile enough to bruise. The pan had only gone next door three nights ago with a mash of carrots and turnip, but she had made herself wait to return it. She told herself she had been busy. She told herself Peter had watched the clock every time she crossed the threshold. She told herself there was no reason for two women to stand shoulder to shoulder at a sink when one could hand a thing back and go home.

The lane looked bleached and hard, the snow stamped into ridges that held the shape of every boot. Smoke sat low. Somebody on the far corner had burned wet coal again and the smell dragged in the throat. Noreen tucked the towel tighter under her arm and kept one hand against the small, hard pull of her belly. It was not pain yet. It was just weight and the day pressing from all sides.

She knocked once on Elsie's back door. There was no answer. She knocked again and heard the slow scrape of a chair leg, then Elsie's voice from inside.

"Door's open. Mind the mat, it's soaked through."

Noreen stepped into the narrow scullery and shut the door fast to keep the heat. Heat was generous for what the house held: one stingy strip from the range and the damp breath off washing that would not dry. Elsie stood by the sink in a cardigan with two buttons missing, sleeves rolled over callused forearms, hair pinned up any way it would stay. Her face looked thinner than last month. Her belly did not.

"I brought your pan," Noreen said, lifting it like proof she had come for a sing...

prev_tail_excerpt:
...d wrote again in the ticket book, pressing so hard the tip snapped. He swore, sharpened it with his pocketknife, and kept writing. He started a fresh page and drew new columns. He tore a strip from the back and tucked it in his shirt pocket.

"I'll do the shop this week," he said without looking up.

"You can't queue from the depot."

"I'll make time."

"With what money?"

"With mine." He shut the book. "And if anything leaves this house, I hear it first."

He went to the pantry and shifted the jars so their labels faced forward in a neat line. He put the tea caddy in the top cupboard and took the key off the nail by the door, then hesitated. He did not lock it yet. He set the key in his pocket anyway.

Later, in bed, he turned his back to her and lay stiff as green timber. Elsie lay on her side facing the wall she shared with Noreen and listened to the night work itself through brick: kettle singing low, floorboard creak, the cough Peter always tried to swallow. After a while she heard Noreen laugh once, quiet and unbelieving at something, then hush herself.

Elsie slipped her hand under the pillow and touched the corner of the tea parcel Noreen had forced on her, folded in wax paper. She had not opened it. She did not know when she would dare.

In the dark, Tom cleared his throat and said, "We start proper tomorrow."

Elsie did not answer. She watched frost feather the bedroom glass again from the inside and thought of the pantry next door, where two hidden tins sat behind a flour sack like contraband, and of how little it took to redraw a border when everyone was hungry.
